narrative: I'm sorry to share this news, but the law school's admission model predicted a 53% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the decision to reject your application—they use this to prioritize students likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. Your profile includes being ranked in the 3rd decile for grades in both your first and third years of school (compared to typical students around the 5th or 6th decile), an LSAT score of 23 (well below the average of about 37), an undergraduate GPA of 2.9 (a bit under the usual 3.2), middle family income, identifying as female and Asian (while most applicants are white and slightly more often male), and planning full-time studies (the norm for nearly everyone). This prediction wasn't random; it was driven most strongly by your third-year decile rank, which pushed the model toward expecting a bar failure because lower ranks like yours are linked to tougher outcomes. Right behind that was your first-year decile, also contributing in the same direction due to its below-average standing. Your LSAT score was the next biggest factor, strongly signaling higher risk since it's so much lower than what succeeds for most. Family income level, racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status followed in influence, each nudging the prediction toward failure in your case, though less powerfully—your GPA edged it that way mildly, while being Asian (less common than white applicants) and middle-income added subtle weight. Overall, your academic metrics, especially those early and later school deciles and LSAT, stood out as the top drivers compared to peers who typically have stronger numbers and get favorable predictions. To boost your future chances, focus on what you can realistically improve: retake the LSAT aiming for a score closer to or above 37, as that's a key lever many students lift successfully. Strengthening any ongoing academic performance could help future decile ranks if you're still in school. Building bar exam prep habits early—like targeted practice in weak areas—might also align better with the profiles of those who pass first time. You're clearly motivated to apply, so channeling that into these steps could shift the odds in your favor for the next round. Hang in there; many overcome similar hurdles with focused effort.
